tldr: Headlines split into two editorial strategies: most outlets lead with the Copernicus temperature record ('hottest June on record / ever'), while Deutsche Welle, Reuters, and Times of India foreground Germany's 5,000+ excess deaths as the primary datum. The Guardian alone pivots to a public-health advisory, naming the UK health service and vulnerable populations in its live-blog headline.

evidence.quote: June heat wave linked to 5,000 excess deaths
Heatwave linked to more than 5,000 deaths in Germany
UK health service urges children and elderly

silences.note: Al Jazeera headline 'Hottest June on record in Western Europe as heatwaves hit continent' delivers the temperature record without invoking deaths or health risk — its death-toll framing appears only in the body text, not the title.

France Info headline is a bare temperature-record announcement in French ('L'Europe occidentale a enregistré son mois de juin le plus chaud jamais observé') with no health, mortality, or policy angle visible from the title.

Geo News headline attributes the record to EU scientists but adds no health or policy framing — a factual certification headline ('say EU scientists') that conceals the body's richer climate-attribution content.
TASS headline cites Copernicus and uses 'hottest in history' rather than 'on record', but carries no health or mortality framing — a pure institutional record announcement.
Bangkok Post delivers a bare record headline; Straits Times adds 'as heatwaves surge' signalling alarm but grounds no distinct codebook frame from the title alone.

single_outlet_findings.finding: 'Germany records over 5,100 heat deaths as Europe reels under record-breaking summer' buries the EU-wide Copernicus finding in a subordinate clause and makes Germany's specific death count the headline lead — the only outlet to localise the story around a named country's mortality tally rather than the regional temperature measurement.

'June 2026 in Western Europe temperatures break record as hottest in history — Copernicus' uses 'in history' rather than 'on record', a subtle hyperbole not used by any other outlet; it also places Copernicus as a dash-attribution at the end, emphasising the institutional endorser over the finding itself.

'Barcelona registers highest temperature in 112 years as UK health service urges children and elderly to take weather seriously' is the only headline in the cluster to invoke a named health authority and name vulnerable populations, converting a climate-record story into an active public-health advisory — a framing visible nowhere else in the corpus.

bottom_line: Headline coverage divides into a temperature-record majority (wires, Asia, France) and a death-toll minority (DW, Reuters, Times of India), with the Guardian uniquely pivoting to a public-health advisory — a three-way editorial split that diverges sharply from the body-text unanimity on the Copernicus finding.
